preFix.title: Painting bare timber with primer and undercoat
preFix.subtitle: Seal and build up bare wood before applying a gloss or eggshell topcoat
preFix.excerpt: How to prepare and paint bare timber: skirting board, architrave, window frame, or door: through the primer and undercoat stages before a gloss or eggshell topcoat, including filling knot holes, sanding between coats, and choosing the right primer for the timber type.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Dulux decorator specification guides. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: Bare timber is porous and resinous. Apply a gloss topcoat directly to it and the paint soaks in unevenly, the grain telegraphs through, and resin from knots bleeds yellow over the following weeks. The primer-and-undercoat sequence is the fix: the primer seals and bonds, the undercoat builds opacity and flatness, and the topcoat provides the finished surface and durability. Cutting a corner on either stage means repeating the work within a year.
Step 1, check for knots and seal them
Examine the timber for knots. Any knot: even a tight dead knot that looks sound: can bleed resin once the heat in a room rises. Brush
knotting solution

onto each knot, extending about 10 mm beyond the knot boundary. Allow to dry: five to ten minutes. Apply a second coat. The shellac dries to an orange-brown film. Do not skip this step on soft woods such as pine, which is the common timber for internal joinery and almost always knotty.
Step 2, fill holes and open grain

Fill any nail holes, splits, or open joints with a flexible interior wood filler. Press the filler into the hole with the 50 mm filling knife, slightly overfilling. Allow to dry: typically 30 to 60 minutes for a small fill. Sand back with 120-grit sandpaper wrapped around the sanding block until flush. Open-grained softwood can be sanded before priming rather than filled: the primer itself will close much of the grain, and the undercoat coat sands smooth.
Step 3, apply the primer
Choose the
wood primer

to match the topcoat system: water-based primer for water-based (acrylic) topcoats; solvent-based primer for traditional oil-based alkyd gloss. Apply with a 50 mm or 25 mm brush depending on the moulding profile, brushing along the grain and then across to ensure the primer fills the surface texture. Work primer into all corners and rebates. Allow to dry fully: minimum two hours for acrylic primer, four hours for oil-based. The surface will feel slightly rough after drying; this is normal.
Step 4, sand the primer coat

Sand the primed surface with 120-grit or 150-grit sandpaper on the sanding block. The primer raises the grain fibres and deposits a slight roughness: this must be sanded off before the undercoat goes on. Work along the grain. The surface should feel smooth to the touch. Wipe off the sanding dust with a damp cloth and allow to dry before applying the undercoat.
Step 5, apply the undercoat
Apply the
undercoat

with the same brush, working along the grain and laying off with light strokes in one direction. The undercoat dries to a flat finish and should cover the primer colour completely. On very pale timber or where the primer is pale, one undercoat is usually sufficient. On dark, stained, or resinous timber, two undercoats may be needed. Allow each undercoat to dry fully before sanding lightly with 150-grit and applying the next.
Step 6, final sand before the topcoat

Sand the final undercoat with 180-grit or 240-grit sandpaper: fine enough to produce a surface that is smooth to the touch without scratching deeply. Work along the grain. Wipe dust. The surface is now ready for the gloss or eggshell topcoat, which is applied following the same brush technique: along the grain, then laid off lightly in one final stroke direction.
Variations

Hardwood joinery (oak, teak, iroko window frames) may need a hardwood primer or a thinned coat of the topcoat used as a primer. Standard softwood primer sits on the surface of dense hardwood rather than penetrating it. Exterior timber needs an exterior primer rated for UV and moisture. The full primer-undercoat-topcoat sequence is non-negotiable outdoors: failure means water ingress and rot, not just a cosmetic problem.

attrs.fix: Sand back to the primer, apply knotting solution to the knot area, allow to dry fully, and reprime and undercoat.
attrs.cause: Knotting solution was not applied or was applied over a damp surface.
attrs.symptom: Yellow bleed marks appear through the paint around knots

attrs.fix: Sand the surface with 150-grit and apply a second thinner coat, laying off with light strokes parallel to the grain.
attrs.cause: Undercoat applied too heavily; not laid off with a light final stroke.
attrs.symptom: Brush marks visible in the undercoat after drying

attrs.fix: Strip back to bare timber, allow to dry for at least a week in a heated room, and repeat the full primer-undercoat sequence.
attrs.cause: Timber was damp when primer was applied; or old paint incompatible with the new system was not removed.
attrs.symptom: Paint film peels at the edge or face of skirting within a few months

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
